preamble: The shift from the lateral approximant [l] to a glottal stop [ʔ] is a form of debuccalization. In several English and Low German dialects, the lateral contact is abandoned in specific phonetic environments (particularly before stops or in syllabic positions), leaving only a glottal gesture.

examples.note: In many AAVE varieties, the lateral is glottalized and then lost before the following dental stop.
While primarily a shift of the /t/, in some Glasgow sub-dialects the entire final syllabic lateral reduces to a glottal catch.
